name: Cedar Native
description: Cedar, particularly Western Red Cedar and other native cedar varieties, holds a place of profound sacredness within numerous Native American cultures across North America. It is more than just a plant; it is a spiritual entity, a conduit to the Creator, and a powerful purifier. Cedar is revered for its protective qualities, believed to ward off negative energies and spirits. Its fragrant smoke is used in smudging ceremonies to cleanse spaces, objects, and individuals, preparing them for prayer, healing, and other sacred activities. The smoke is thought to carry prayers to the Great Spirit. The evergreen nature of cedar symbolizes longevity, strength, and resilience. It is often incorporated into traditional regalia, woven into baskets, and used in construction of ceremonial structures. Different tribes have unique stories and beliefs surrounding cedar, but its central role as a sacred plant remains consistent. Cedar branches are sometimes placed on the floor of sweat lodges or burned in sacred fires. It is believed to attract good spirits and promote healing on physical, emotional, and spiritual levels. The giving of cedar as a gift is a sign of respect and friendship. The wood is also used to craft masks and other ritual objects. The scent of cedar is considered calming and grounding, helping to connect individuals with the natural world and their ancestors. The tree itself is seen as a relative, a living being deserving of respect and gratitude. Harvesting cedar is often done with prayer and offerings, acknowledging the tree's sacrifice and ensuring its continued abundance. Overharvesting is strongly discouraged, and sustainable practices are emphasized to maintain the balance of nature. Cedar continues to play a vital role in maintaining cultural traditions and spiritual well-being for many Native American communities.
subtitle: Sacred purifier, protector, prayer carrier.

keyMyths.title: Cedar as a Protector
keyMyths.description: Cedar shields people from harmful spirits, creating a safe space for healing and connection. Its smoke is a barrier against negativity, allowing positive energy to flourish.

sources.author: Paula Gunn Allen

sources.description: Explores the role of plants and nature in Native American spirituality.

extendedContent.title: Cedar in Ceremony and Ritual
extendedContent.content: Cedar plays a central role in many Native American ceremonies and rituals. The fragrant smoke produced by burning cedar is believed to cleanse spaces, objects, and individuals of negative energies, preparing them for spiritual work. Smudging, the practice of using smoke for purification, is common, with cedar often burned alongside sage, sweetgrass, and tobacco. The smoke is wafted over individuals, sacred objects, and around the perimeter of a space to create a protective barrier. Cedar is also used in sweat lodge ceremonies, where its branches are placed on the hot stones to release its purifying aroma, aiding in physical and spiritual cleansing. The act of offering cedar to the fire is a sign of respect and gratitude to the Creator and the spirit world, inviting blessings and guidance.

extendedContent.title: Cedar as Medicine and Healing
extendedContent.content: Beyond its spiritual significance, cedar possesses medicinal properties recognized and utilized by Native American healers for generations. The leaves and bark of cedar trees contain compounds with antiseptic, anti-inflammatory, and expectorant qualities. Cedar tea is traditionally used to treat coughs, colds, and respiratory ailments, helping to clear congestion and soothe irritated airways. The oil extracted from cedar can be applied topically to treat skin conditions, wounds, and muscle aches. Furthermore, the aromatic properties of cedar are believed to have a calming effect on the mind and body, reducing stress and promoting relaxation. The use of cedar in traditional medicine reflects a holistic understanding of health, recognizing the interconnectedness of physical, emotional, and spiritual well-being. Knowledge of these medicinal applications is carefully preserved and passed down through generations.

extendedContent.title: Cedar in Native American Art and Craft
extendedContent.content: The versatility of cedar extends beyond its spiritual and medicinal applications, finding expression in various forms of Native American art and craft. The durable and pliable bark of cedar trees is used for weaving baskets, mats, and clothing. Cedar wood is favored for carving masks, totem poles, and other ceremonial objects, its reddish hue and fine grain lending itself to intricate designs. In the Pacific Northwest, monumental totem poles carved from Western Red Cedar stand as testaments to ancestral lineage, clan identity, and spiritual beliefs. These towering sculptures often depict mythical beings, animal spirits, and historical events, serving as visual narratives that connect the past to the present. The use of cedar in art and craft is not merely functional but symbolic, imbuing objects with spiritual power and cultural significance.

habitat: Various cedar species are native to North America, thriving in diverse environments from coastal rainforests to mountainous regions.
shortDescription: Sacred to many Native American tribes, cedar is a spiritual entity known for purification and protection. Its smoke is used in ceremonies for healing and blessings.
